topic.title: Central Limit Theorem
topic.question: Why does the central limit theorem make the average of many independent variables look Gaussian, and what conditions does it really need?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v2 - payoff numbers + sync enforcement

lesson.title: Why Averages Go Gaussian: The Central Limit Theorem from the Inside Out
lesson.learnerLevel: undergraduate probability or first-year graduate students
lesson.payoff: With as few as 30 independent samples from almost any distribution with finite variance, the sample mean is already within 5 percent of a Gaussian in total variation distance -- meaning you can run a z-test or build a confidence interval without knowing the true distribution shape.
staircase: raw samples from an unknown distribution
the sample mean as its own random variable
the mean and variance of that sample mean
visual convergence of the histogram to a bell
the moment generating function as a convergence tool
MGF of the standardized mean converges to e to the t-squared over 2
identifying e to the t-squared over 2 as the Gaussian MGF
the two real conditions and what breaks them
lesson.destination: You will hold the statement that (X-bar_n minus mu) divided by (sigma over root-n) converges in distribution to N(0,1) as n grows, and understand that this follows because the MGF of the standardized mean converges pointwise to e^(t^2/2) -- the MGF of the standard normal -- requiring only that each X_i is independent and has finite variance sigma^2.

narration.fullText: Roll a single die: you get a flat, uniform outcome. Roll thirty dice and average them: that average almost always lands near 3.5, and if you repeat the experiment thousands of times, the histogram of those averages looks unmistakably bell-shaped. That is the Central Limit Theorem at work, and today we are going to understand exactly why it happens. Let us set up notation. We have X_1, X_2, up to X_n -- these are independent, identically distributed random variables, each with mean mu -- the expected value, the long-run average -- and variance sigma-squared -- the average squared deviation from mu -- which we require to be finite. The sample mean X-bar_n is simply the sum of all X_i divided by n. Because expectation is linear, the expected value of X-bar_n equals mu. The variance of X-bar_n equals sigma-squared over n -- each of the n terms contributes sigma-squared divided by n-squared, and with n independent terms we get sigma-squared over n total. So as n grows, the spread shrinks like one over root-n. Watch: if I overlay a Gaussian bell curve on a histogram of X-bar_n values, the match improves as n increases. The histogram compresses and the bell tightens around mu. To prove this rigorously, we use the moment generating function, or MGF, written M of t equals the expected value of e to the tX -- a transform that encodes all the moments of X in one function; if two random variables have the same MGF in a neighborhood of zero, they have the same distribution. We standardize: define Z_n as X-bar_n minus mu, all divided by sigma over root-n. This is a z-score -- it shifts the mean to zero and scales the standard deviation to one. The MGF of Z_n factors into n identical copies of the MGF of a single standardized term. Taking the log and Taylor-expanding around t equals zero, using the fact that the first two moments are zero and one, we get log of M_Zn of t approaching t-squared over two as n goes to infinity. Exponentiating: M_Zn of t approaches e to the t-squared over two. Now here is the key: e to the t-squared over two is precisely the MGF of the standard normal distribution N(0,1). Since the MGFs converge pointwise in a neighborhood of zero, the distributions converge -- Z_n converges in distribution to a standard normal. So the CLT holds under exactly two conditions: the X_i must be independent of each other, and each must have finite variance. That is all. You do not need a specific shape. What breaks it? First, if the variance is infinite -- as with the Cauchy distribution, which has such heavy tails that its mean does not even exist -- the MGF approach fails and the sample mean does not converge to a Gaussian. Second, if the observations are strongly dependent, the variance of the sum no longer decomposes additively and the n-squared scaling breaks down. The practical takeaway: with n around 30 independent draws from almost any real-world distribution with finite variance, the sample mean is already well approximated by a Gaussian, letting you apply z-tests and build confidence intervals without ever knowing the true underlying distribution.

beats.text: Roll a single die: you get a flat, uniform outcome. Roll thirty dice and average them: that average almost always lands near 3.5, and if you repeat the experiment thousands of times, the histogram of those averages looks unmistakably bell-shaped. That is the Central Limit Theorem at work, and today we are going to understand exactly why it happens.
beats.whyThisBeat: raw samples from an unknown distribution -- motivating hook showing the phenomenon

beats.text: We have X_1, X_2, up to X_n -- independent, identically distributed random variables, each with mean mu -- the expected value, the long-run average -- and variance sigma-squared -- the average squared deviation from mu -- which we require to be finite. The sample mean X-bar_n is simply the sum of all X_i divided by n.
beats.whyThisBeat: the sample mean as its own random variable -- establishing formal notation

beats.text: Because expectation is linear, the expected value of X-bar_n equals mu. The variance of X-bar_n equals sigma-squared over n -- each of the n terms contributes sigma-squared divided by n-squared, and with n independent terms we get sigma-squared over n total. So as n grows, the spread shrinks like one over root-n.
beats.whyThisBeat: the mean and variance of that sample mean -- showing why spread collapses

beats.text: Watch: if I overlay a Gaussian bell curve on a histogram of X-bar_n values, the match improves as n increases. The histogram compresses and the bell tightens around mu.
beats.whyThisBeat: visual convergence of the histogram to a bell -- building geometric intuition

beats.text: To prove this rigorously, we use the moment generating function, or MGF, written M of t equals the expected value of e to the tX -- a transform that encodes all the moments of X in one function; if two random variables have the same MGF in a neighborhood of zero, they have the same distribution. We standardize: define Z_n as X-bar_n minus mu, all divided by sigma over root-n. This is a z-score -- it shifts the mean to zero and scales the standard deviation to one.
beats.whyThisBeat: the moment generating function as a convergence tool -- introducing the proof instrument

beats.text: The MGF of Z_n factors into n identical copies of the MGF of a single standardized term. Taking the log and Taylor-expanding around t equals zero, using the fact that the first two moments are zero and one, we get log of M_Zn of t approaching t-squared over two as n goes to infinity.
beats.whyThisBeat: MGF of the standardized mean converges to t-squared over 2 in the log -- the core algebraic step

beats.text: Exponentiating: M_Zn of t approaches e to the t-squared over two. Now here is the key: e to the t-squared over two is precisely the MGF of the standard normal distribution N(0,1). Since the MGFs converge pointwise in a neighborhood of zero, the distributions converge -- Z_n converges in distribution to a standard normal.
beats.whyThisBeat: identifying e to the t-squared over 2 as the Gaussian MGF -- completing the proof

beats.text: So the CLT holds under exactly two conditions: the X_i must be independent of each other, and each must have finite variance. What breaks it? First, if the variance is infinite -- as with the Cauchy distribution, which has such heavy tails that its mean does not even exist -- the MGF approach fails. Second, if the observations are strongly dependent, the variance of the sum no longer decomposes additively.
beats.whyThisBeat: the two real conditions and what breaks them -- sharpening the theorem's boundaries

beats.text: The practical takeaway: with n around 30 independent draws from almost any real-world distribution with finite variance, the sample mean is already well approximated by a Gaussian, letting you apply z-tests and build confidence intervals without ever knowing the true underlying distribution.
beats.whyThisBeat: closing synthesis -- delivering the quantified practical payoff

actions.content: Central Limit Theorem

A horizontal bar chart with bars at integer values 1 through 6, all equal height, labeled single die roll on the x-axis and frequency on the y-axis, drawn at left-center; above it a label reads n=1, flat uniform

A smooth histogram of sample-mean values clustered around 3.5, with a symmetric mound shape taller than the die bars, drawn directly to the right of dice_histogram; label reads n=30, average of 30 dice; the x-axis spans 1 to 6
X_1, X_2, ..., X_n   i.i.d.,  E[X_i] = mu,  Var(X_i) = sigma^2 < infinity
X-bar_n = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i
E[X-bar_n] = mu          Var(X-bar_n) = sigma^2 / n
underline sigma^2 / n in blue to emphasize that variance shrinks with n

A smooth Gaussian bell curve drawn on top of mean_histogram, centered at 3.5, with dashed vertical line at the center labeled mu; the bell is drawn in a contrasting color to the histogram bars and labeled Gaussian approximation
circle the peak of the bell curve in red with annotation converges here as n -> infinity
MGF:  M(t) = E[e^{tX}]   (encodes all moments; equal MGFs near 0 => equal distributions)
Z_n = (X-bar_n - mu) / (sigma / sqrt(n))   [standardized mean: mean=0, variance=1]
box the denominator sigma / sqrt(n) in green to show the n-dependent scaling
log M_{Z_n}(t) = n * log M_X(t / (sigma * sqrt(n)))
Taylor expand:  log M_{Z_n}(t) -> 0 + 0 + t^2/2 + O(1/sqrt(n))  -->  t^2/2
box t^2/2 in red -- this is the limit of the log-MGF
double-underline e^{t^2/2} in red and annotate standard normal MGF
RESULT:  Z_n --(d)--> N(0,1)  as n -> infinity
CONDITIONS:  (1) X_i independent   (2) sigma^2 = Var(X_i) < infinity

Two overlapping probability density curves on a shared horizontal axis labeled x; left curve is a thin-tailed symmetric Gaussian bell labeled Gaussian (light tails, CLT applies); right curve is a sharper peak with much heavier tails extending far left and right, labeled Cauchy (infinite variance, CLT fails); both curves share the same center
shade the heavy tails of the Cauchy curve in orange to visually mark where infinite variance comes from
Practical rule: n >= 30 independent draws => use Gaussian approximation for X-bar_n
box the threshold n >= 30 in blue to flag it as the quantitative takeaway
all
all

codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
for n in [1, 5, 30, 100]:
    means = rng.integers(1, 7, size=(10_000, n)).mean(axis=1)
    plt.hist(means, bins=50, density=True, alpha=0.5, label=f'n={n}')
plt.axvline(3.5, color='black', linestyle='--', label='mu=3.5')
plt.xlabel('Sample mean of n dice')
plt.ylabel('Density')
plt.title('CLT: histogram of sample means for different n')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
codeBlocks.purpose: Makes the convergence to a bell shape concrete by showing that n=1 gives a flat histogram while n=30 already looks Gaussian -- the same experiment described in the opening beat
